thread.title: Regional homeless out of sight, out of mind for WA government, say locals
thread.title_full: Regional homeless out of sight, out of mind for WA government, say locals

author: Bridget McArthur
title: Regional homeless out of sight, out of mind for WA government, say locals
text: [Bridget McArthur](/news/bridget-mcarthur/101975508)and [Dunja Karagic](/news/dunja-karagic/13833228) In short: Empty buildings used as makeshift refuges by squatters are being locked up or demolished, forcing people back onto the streets. While the state government says it is focused on funding long-term housing, residents in regional cities say emergency measures are needed before new homes are built. What's next? A Scottish support service says WA could consider temporarily housing rough sleepers in vacant hotel rooms and short-term rentals — a solution that has been successfully rolled out in Glasgow. Six months ago, Robert Denison finally got some rest. After years of shifting endlessly between the bus station and various shop fronts in Bunbury, WA's largest regional city, he left the CBD and set up camp in an abandoned boat club overlooking the Preston River. There, he has faced significantly less harassment from authorities and some members of the public. "The biggest stress I had about being on the streets was waiting to be told when I had to leave that spot and had to try and find somewhere else," he said. "It's gotten easier now actually because there's no stress … and you can't argue with the view." But Mr Denison could soon find himself back on the streets. The boat club is facing demolition after an audit of council-owned buildings raised concerns over its condition. The City of Bunbury is engaging local service providers to try to help those who have been staying there after a request for crisis accommodation funding was rejected by the state government. Housing Minister John Carey said the government was focused on building more long-term housing. But Councillor Michelle Steck said homes were not being built quickly enough. "The reality is that people are living in unsatisfactory accommodation … but there's simply nowhere for them to go," she said. "We want something now that we can put up very quickly." Councillor Steck said there was plenty of empty land in Bunbury where kit homes could be set up to house homeless people. Groundhog Day Jeremy Wood said it was not the first time the scenario facing Mr Denison had played out in Bunbury. One year ago, he and his girlfriend were squatting at an abandoned building on Charles Street when a contractor turned up and told him they had been instructed to knock the place down. Mr Wood said they were given five minutes to leave. "I couldn't get everything out. They flattened the building," he said. "Now it's just an empty block." Local advocate Mike Fenton said the property had a long history of serving homeless people. Most recently, it was run by support service Accordwest who, a few years before it was demolished, had been trying to secure funding to house at-risk older women there. WA's Department of Communities, which owns the lot, said the building had been "unable to be refurbished to be brought up to modern standard". The department was not able to clarify why it was unable to be refurbished but said the site was being retained for future development. While the boat club is council-owned, the land it is on belongs to the state, meaning if it is demolished it will become another empty state-owned lot. Mr Wood has since secured a roof over his head through the state-funded Housing Choices program, which has supported more than 40 people in Bunbury since its inception in 2021, according to the state government. But he said many of his friends remained on the streets. A 2023 parliamentary inquiry found Bunbury only had two emergency accommodation units, both of which were exclusively available to families. It also found there was no out-of-hours crisis service in Bunbury. Meanwhile, the Department of Communities had funded six crisis and accommodation services in Perth. Regional services overwhelmed Services in the Kimberley region have also been pushing the state government for emergency solutions. Housing support worker Deirdre Lechleitner said people often came to Broome from remote Indigenous communities to access medical services at the hospital and found themselves with nowhere to stay. "It's not so much that no-one is voicing the problems or issues that we're facing; it's more [we're not] being heard," the Centacare team leader said. "I think it's just at a point where everyone's quite overwhelmed." Ms Lechleitner said local service providers agreed the city needed a night shelter. The number of people sleeping rough in WA was more than 2,300 at the time of the 2021 Census — more than double the number recorded in the previous Census. Data from the Australian Institute of Health and Welfare showed that more than half of people who accessed homelessness services in WA in 2022–23 were in regional or remote areas despite only making up about 20 per cent of the state's population. In regional WA, real-time statistics remain limited but service providers in Bunbury recorded more than 40 per cent more rough sleepers in September since the same time last year, according to the Advance to Zero monthly by-name list published by the Australian Alliance to End Homelessness. In Geraldton — the only other regional area where such data is recorded — the number of people experiencing homelessness had doubled. The ABC has spoken to several organisations in other parts of regional WA reporting similar increases. The state government told the ABC it had invested a record $3.2 billion in housing and homelessness measures. However, WA Alliance to End Homelessness (WAAEH) analysis of Productivity Commission data showed total net real expenditure on social housing and homelessness services in WA went down by more than 20 per cent from 2016–17 to 2022–23. An overseas solution to a local crisis Last month, WAAEH hosted a summit and invited organisations from other states and countries to share success stories, including Simon Community Scotland. The Scottish support service has been using hotels to help tackle rough sleeping in the short term. CEO Lorraine McGrath said the strategy had contributed to cutting the number of people sleeping on Glasgow's streets to less than 40 in any given week. "It might not be in the nicest hotel, but it will be a hotel room," she said. "Then the following day there'll be follow-up with you to find out what your needs are and what the next steps can be for you. "You have an absolute statutory right to that offer." Short-stay accommodation is something many regional areas have in spades. Averaged across seven days in November, the ABC found there were 23 accommodation listings available on a popular booking platform each night. In Broome, there were 295. The state government said it already provided flexible funding to service providers that could be used to put rough sleepers up in short-term accommodation like hotel rooms. It said it was up to providers how they spent their funds. State Greens MP Brad Pettitt said he planned to push parliament to develop a homelessness strategy tailored to the regions. "When we see the Department of Communities so quick to demolish and frankly being so slow to build over the past few years, it makes my blood boil," he said. "A less than ideal house, a substandard house is better than no house at all." While debate rages over what is needed and who should fund it, homeless people across the state continue with the day-to-day task of surviving. "[There's] not much point getting upset or anything about it," Mr Denison said. "It's not going to change anything; it's just going to make things worse for myself really, getting angry for no reason." He said his brother had bought him a tent and he had started contacting caravan parks.
